topic: Theme 3: Communication and the World Around Us
subject: spanish

items.q: Where do most descriptive adjectives go relative to the noun?
items.misconception: Before the noun, as in English.
items.random_wrong: Either position with the same meaning.
items.why: Students apply English order. In Spanish, descriptive adjectives typically FOLLOW the noun. Some adjectives precede (limiting adjectives like buen, gran) and can change meaning - gran amigo (great friend) vs amigo grande (big friend).

items.q: Why does 'qué' have an accent in '¿Qué quieres?' but not always elsewhere?
items.correct: The accent distinguishes interrogative/exclamative 'qué' from relative 'que'.
items.why: Students see two spellings of the 'same word'. Spanish uses written accents to distinguish homophones with different functions: qué (question/exclamation) vs que (that/which - relative).

items.correct: Could be his/her/your(formal)/their brother - context determines which.
items.why: Students assume su = his. SU is one of Spanish's most context-dependent words. Means his, her, your (usted/ustedes), or their. Speakers often clarify with 'de él', 'de ella', 'de usted' if ambiguous: 'el hermano de él'.

items.q: Which sentence correctly says 'I get on well with my sister'?
items.correct: Me llevo bien con mi hermana.
items.misconception: Me llevo bueno con mi hermana.
items.random_wrong: Yo llevo bien con mi hermana.
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Me llevo bien con mi hermana.. “Me llevo bueno con mi hermana.” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which is the correct use of ser/estar: 'I am tired'?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Estoy cansado.. “Soy cansado.” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many forms does the Spanish adjective 'simpático' have (masc sing, masc plur, fem sing, fem plur)?
items.why: The real figure is 4 forms. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
